docs.text: by Wolfgang · May 13, 2026

docs.title: EasyBus Lighting Systems Manual
docs.text: Wolfgang Haring e.U. Company Register Number: FN 312421 v (Regional Court Wr. Neustadt) Kammanngasse 7-9A/8, A-2700 Wiener Neustadt Phone: +49 30 6098490 431

docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Dear Customer,

We are very pleased that you have chosen one of our products. If you have any questions or suggestions, please do not hesitate to contact us by email at info@kraftwerk.shop or visit our website www.kraftwerk.shop. There you will always find the latest user manuals, software updates, and helpful videos.

We wish you much enjoyment with your new product!

docs.title: Safety Instructions
docs.text:
• Read this manual carefully and pay special attention to the warnings marked in red.
  • Changes to the wiring may only be made when the device is switched off.
  • Ensure that no unexpected movements of the model can cause damage during commissioning and configuration (e.g., remove drive wheels from the ground).
  • Protect all electronic components from moisture and oil.
  • Do not route data-carrying cables (e.g., antenna or bus cables) near power cables (motor, battery, digital servos) to avoid interference.
  • Ensure a sufficient and stable power supply. Cheap BECs or speed controllers labeled "BEC 5V/1A" often do not provide the necessary voltage quality.
  • Replace damaged components and cables immediately.
  • Operate the components only within the specified technical specifications.
  • Disconnect the battery after driving, especially important for LiPo batteries.
  • Avoid short circuits and reverse polarity.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Model building is becoming increasingly demanding, and with it, the requirements for electronics are also rising. Modern electronics must be powerful, compact, expandable, configurable, updatable, and above all user-friendly. We have implemented these requirements for truck functional model building with our EasyBus system.

The KLM 4/16 is not only a standalone lighting system with numerous functions and adjustment options but also the control center of our EasyBus system. Pre-assembled lighting boards can be connected via the EasyBus, which are equipped with wired LEDs for side indicators, position lights, and other functions depending on the model.

Commissioning is simple and requires no PC: plug in the boards, learn the channels, and get started – plug & play!

For individual adjustments such as brightness, switch-on and switch-off speeds, blink frequencies, or brake light sensitivity, the system can be connected to a PC via USB. The free Kraftwerk ControlPanel automatically detects all connected modules and enables intuitive configuration via mouse. Settings are saved directly in the module and can be backed up, restored, or reset to factory defaults.

Our EasyBus systems offer more than just light control: they also include universal switch expansions, servo controls, speed controllers, as well as infrared transmitters and receivers.

docs.title: Product Description
docs.text:
The KLM 4/16 is connected to up to four channels of the receiver:

  • Throttle channel: Control of reverse and brake lights
  • Steering channel: Control of cornering lights and automatic turn signal reset
  • Two additional channels: Control of lighting and auxiliary functions

Alternatively, the KLM 4/16 can be controlled via CPPM, I-BUS, or S-BUS (if supported by the receiver) or another data interface.

Of the 16 switching outputs, eight are high-current outputs (up to 500 mA), the other eight have integrated 180-ohm resistors and can directly drive LEDs. Each output is freely configurable in function (e.g., parking light, brake light, turn signal) and behavior (brightness, on/off speed).

Output 8 can be used as an infrared transmitter; the transmitting diode is included.

The four servo outputs are also flexibly adjustable, e.g., direction of rotation, end positions, and speed.

The KLM 4/16 is compatible with all common speed controllers, sound modules, and remote control systems. It also supports multiswitch modules from Graupner and Robbe as well as our control pads for basic and additional functions and the Light & Sound Pad.

docs.title: Technical Data KLM 4/16
docs.text: Parameter Description Power supply Via receiver power supply (BEC of the speed controller, typically 5-6 V) Max. current consumption 30 mA Voltage at the "+" outputs BEC voltage (typically 5-6 V) Number of servo inputs 4 Number of analog inputs 6 Alternative inputs CPPM, I-BUS, S-BUS, SUMD Supported multiswitches ROBBE, Graupner Supported speed controllers All Supported sound modules All Supported remote control systems All Number of servo outputs 4 (adjustable) Number of switching outputs 16 (adjustable) Current rating outputs 1-8 500 mA each for 30 seconds Current rating outputs 9-16 20 mA each (180 ohm resistors integrated) Dimensions 52 x 36 x 13 mm

docs.title: Commissioning
docs.text: No special steps are required for commissioning the KraftwerK EasyBus systems. However, we recommend observing the following checklist:

docs.title: Connection to the Receiver
docs.text:
image6.png

Before connecting the KLM 4/16 to the receiver, please check the assignment of the receiver outputs. Use a servo to test each output for correct function. Note the assignment.

Note: It is advisable to prop up the model to prevent unintended movement.

Connect the servo cables for steering and throttle to the corresponding receiver outputs. The speed controller and steering servo are connected to the servo outputs of the KLM. Channels K1 and K2 can be used optionally.

Tip: The connection of the throttle and/or steering channel can be omitted if brake, reverse light, and/or indicators are fed in analog (see section 11.5).

Tip: Alternatively, the speed controller and/or steering servo can also be connected directly to the receiver with a Y-cable to exclude interference from the KLM.

docs.title: Connection of the Lighting Boards
docs.text: All expansion or lighting boards can be connected directly or via distribution boards to the bus outputs of the light assistant.

docs.title: Learning the Channels
docs.text:
image6.png

Learning the channels is essential for the proper operation of the EasyBus systems. After every change to the remote control system (e.g., deflections, rotation directions, center positions), the channels must be relearned.

Servos and motors controlled by the KraftwerK system must be adjusted via the ControlPanel. Do not use the menu of your remote control system!

  1. Turn on the remote control and prop up the model so that no drive wheels touch the ground.
  2. Turn on the model. After the green LED flashes twice, move the throttle and steering sticks to the end positions for five seconds until the red LED lights up for about one second.
  3. Return the sticks to the center position.

The LED signals during learning:

  • 1x blink: Throttle stick – full throttle, then full brake
  • 2x blink: Steering stick – full left, then full right
  • 3x blink: Additional channel K1 – full left, then full right
  • 4x blink: Additional channel K2 – full up, then full down

After all sticks return to the center position, the red LED lights continuously, signaling the end of the learning process. The system restarts automatically and indicates readiness with the double flashing of the green LED.

docs.title: Light Functions
docs.text: Function Description Left indicator / Right indicator With automatic reset, optionally automatic with steering angle (see section 9.3). Flash frequency adjustable in system settings. Hazard lights Automatically activatable when reversing. Parking light   image9.png Parking light & low beam Optionally with xenon effect for the low beam. Parking light & high beam Optionally with xenon effect for the high beam. Parking light, low & high beam Optionally with xenon effect for low or high beam. Low beam Optionally with xenon effect. Low beam, high beam Optionally with xenon effect for low or high beam. High beam Optionally with xenon effect. Fog lights Expert function: front light possible with all combinations. Daytime running lights Always on, can be dimmed when indicator and/or parking light are on. Cornering light / curve light Active when indicator is operated or steering exceeds threshold (see system settings). Optional with fog lights. Brake light Intelligent control with adjustable sensitivity and afterglow duration. Reverse light Simple forward/reverse controller or with separate brake/reverse. Tail light & brake light / rear fog light All combinations possible. American indicator Parking light and indicator via one LED. American tail light Parking light, indicator and brake light via one LED. All-round light On/Off Switches output to 100% when all-round light is activated. Single switch / double switch Switches outputs depending on selected function and brightness. Multi-flasher Expert function with adjustable start delay, pulse count and pauses. Running light Configuration of a running light over multiple outputs. KnightRider Well-known running light effect with adjustable brightness and speed.

docs.title: Servo Functions
docs.text: Function Description Proportional Control of a servo or motor controller with a proportional channel. Proportional switched Control with proportional channel, active only with a certain switch function. Two positions Servo moves between two positions depending on switch function. Three positions Servo moves between three positions, controlled by two switch functions. Saddle plate / gearshift Specifically for model functions like saddle plate or gearshift.

docs.title: Motor Functions
docs.text: Function Description Proportional Control of a motor with a proportional channel. Proportional switched Motor control only when switch function is active. Drop legs / dump bed / tipper Motor control for specific model functions.

docs.title: Control Variants / Receiver Type
docs.text: The control variant is selected in the system settings of the KLM 4/16 under "Receiver Type".

docs.title: Normal 4xPWM
docs.text: The KLM 4/16 is connected to up to four channels of the receiver. The channels must be manually or with the ControlPanel learned in order to define center positions, end points, and rotation directions.

docs.title: CPPM
docs.text: If your receiver offers a CPPM output, it can be connected to the CPPM input of the KLM. If necessary, activate the CPPM output on the receiver (see the remote control system manual). The channel assignment is done via the LiveData view in the ControlPanel.

docs.title: I-BUS
docs.text: For receivers with I-BUS output (e.g., Turnigy), the procedure is analogous to CPPM.

docs.title: S-BUS
docs.text: For receivers with S-BUS output (e.g., FUTABA), the procedure is analogous to CPPM.

docs.title: Control Variants / Receiver Types
docs.text: The control type can be selected in the system settings of the KLM 4/16 under "Receiver Type".

docs.title: Normal 4xPWM
docs.text: The KLM 4/16 is connected to up to four channels of the receiver. The channels must be manually or via the ControlPanel learned to define center positions, end points, and rotation directions.

docs.title: CPPM
docs.text: If your receiver offers a CPPM output (summary signal), it can be connected to the CPPM input of the KLM. Activate the CPPM output on the receiver if necessary (see the remote control system manual). The channel assignment is done via the live data view in the ControlPanel.

docs.title: I-BUS
docs.text: For receivers with an I-BUS output (e.g., Turnigy), this can be connected to the I-BUS input of the KLM. Activation on the receiver is required. The further steps correspond to those of CPPM.

docs.title: S-BUS
docs.text: For receivers with an S-BUS output (e.g., FUTABA), the same procedure applies as with CPPM and I-BUS.

docs.title: Speed Controller Type
docs.text:
The KLM 4/16 is compatible with all speed controllers. For correct control of brake and reverse lights, the speed controller type must be appropriately set in the system settings:

  • Simple: Reverse starts immediately when pulling the stick backward.
  • Brake Separate: Brake first, then reverse by pulling again.
  • Standard Cruise Control: Transition from deceleration directly into reverse without an extra switching channel.
  • Servonaut Cruise Control: Gear shifting via K1, servo output set to "Proportional function K1."
  • Servonaut Neutral: Like Servonaut Cruise Control, but with neutral position.
  • Servonaut K40: Special mode for K40 speed controllers.
  • Analog Brake/Reverse Light: Feeding existing switching outputs of the speed controller.

docs.title: Intelligent Brake Light
docs.text: The brake light switches on already during deceleration (not only when reversing). Sensitivity and afterglow duration are adjustable.

docs.title: Steering Type
docs.text: By default, the turn signal must be switched on manually and switches off automatically. Alternatively, the automatic turn signal can be activated during cornering. The "Turn Signal Threshold" defines the activation threshold.

docs.title: Corner Light Type
docs.text: The behavior of the corner light can be controlled via the parameter "Corner Light Type." The options are self-explanatory.

docs.title: Sound Module
docs.text: Sound modules can be operated directly on the receiver or controlled via the EasyBus system. For this purpose, they are connected to two servo outputs (KLM 4/16, KSB 2, or KSB 4).

docs.title: Servonaut SMT Compatible
docs.text: One channel for throttle information, the second for start/stop and horn.

docs.title: Programmable Sound Modules (e.g., BEIER USM-RC)
docs.text: Advanced control with an additional horn. Settings are made in the sound module.

docs.title: Control of the Sound Module
docs.text: When using the Light & Sound control pad, no further configuration is necessary. The horn button controls the horn, the level button starts or stops the engine. Additional channels K1 to K10 can be assigned by the user.

docs.title: Engine Starts Immediately
docs.text:
Depending on the sound module, the system setting "Engine starts immediately [active]" must be adjusted:

  • Sound module starts engine immediately: check the box.
  • Engine must be started manually: uncheck the box.

Caution: The drive motor is active in any case!

docs.title: Lights Flicker
docs.text:
Each lighting board and switch extension (KLB) has system settings for "Start effect" (flickering at start) and "Engine stop light reduction" (dimming when engine stops). Use the latter option only if no components with integrated electronics are connected.

The duration of the flickering can be adjusted with "Engine start duration" to match the start sound duration.

docs.title: Connection Diagrams
docs.text:
• Actros 3363 with standard cruise control

image36.png - Actros 3363 with standard cruise control and infrared connection for trailer

image37.png - Actros 3363 with cruise control

image38.png - Actros 3363 brake/reverse light from cruise control

image39.png - Actros 3363 brake/reverse light and turn signal from cruise control

image40.png

docs.title: System Settings
docs.text:
System settings apply to the entire module or system (e.g., blink interval, receiver type, brake light type). You open these via the gear icon or the menu “System” → “System Settings”.

image41.png

image42.png

Name Description Start Delay [s] Delays system start to account for startup times of the remote control. Learning Mode [active] Activates/deactivates manual learning mode (throttle and steering sticks at end positions). ControlPanel learning remains active. Infrared [on] Switches output 8 to infrared mode. Blinker On/Off Defines on and off time of the blinker in 10 ms steps. Receiver Type See section 8 “Control Variants / Receiver Type”. ESC Type See section 9.1 “ESC Type”. Hazard Lights when Reversing Automatic activation of hazard lights when reversing. Brake Light Sensitivity Sensitivity of the intelligent brake light. Brake Light Afterglow Duration of brake light afterglow. Steering Type See section 9.3 “Steering Type”. Blinker Threshold Threshold for automatic blinker activation. Cornering Light Type See section 0 “Cornering Light Type”. Motor Runs Immediately [active] See section 10 “Sound Module”. Motor Start Duration Duration of motor start flickering. Pad All On/Off Configuration of the “All On/Off” button of the control pads. Pad Light & Sound High Beam / Fog Lights See Light & Sound Control Pad manual. Assignment K1 – K12 See section 13.6 “Custom”.

docs.title: Control of Functions Light – Driving – Additional
docs.text:
The standard control is via four proportional channels (throttle left, steering right). Channel K2 switches between the function groups Light, Driving, and Additional.

image43.png

Learning the channels:

  1. Throttle – Brake
  2. Left – Right
  3. K1 left – right
  4. K2 up – down

image44.png

docs.title: Switching Light Functions
docs.text:
• Parking light, low beam: K2 up + tap K1 briefly left
  • High beam: K2 up + tap K1 briefly right (only if parking light is on)
  • Fog lights, rear fog light: K2 up + tap K1 long right (only if parking light is on)
  • Blinker left/right: K2 middle + tap K1 briefly left/right
  • Hazard lights: K2 middle + tap K1 long right
  • Flash-to-pass: K2 middle + tap K1 long left (after first activation, K1 left can be controlled directly)
  • Additional functions: K2 down + move K1 to end position (left/right) briefly or long

image45.png

to

image54.png

docs.title: Pad Basic or Additional Functions
docs.text:
Pads encode button presses into upward and downward movements. The KLM decodes these and controls the functions. Pads are connected to the remote control transmitter and connected to K1 or K2 of the KLM.

To learn the channels, the pad must be put into setup mode (simultaneous pressing of the two upper right setup buttons). Alternatively, use the “Learn Channels” assistant in the ControlPanel.

image61.png

image63.png

docs.title: Pad Light & Sound
docs.text:
Controls functions depending on button press with deflections from +100 to -100%. Connection and operation are analogous to basic and additional functions.

image65.png

Functions are divided into short, long, and additional layers. Flash-to-pass, horn, and other functions are activated by long press and can then be controlled with short presses.

After two seconds of inactivity, the original assignment is restored.

image67.png

docs.title: Multiswitch Robbe/Graupner
docs.text:
Both protocols are automatically detected. Multiswitch values can be assigned to channels K3 to K12 in the Live Data assistant and custom assigned.

image68.png

docs.title: Custom
docs.text:
Each channel (except throttle and steering) can be assigned individually. K1 and K2 support full and half deflections, K3 to K12 only full deflections.

image69.png

image70.png

Functions can be defined as “Continuous”, “Memory short”, or “Memory long”. Examples include light functions, horn, fifth wheel coupling, supports, dump bed, ramp, start, shift, servo1/2, etc.

image71.png

docs.title: General
docs.text:
When starting the ControlPanel, the model must be operational: battery charged and connected, model and remote control switched on and bound, servos movable.

The system is automatically detected and all modules are displayed.

image72.png

image73.png

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
By clicking on a module, you reach the output overview. There you can assign names, set on and off speeds, and configure additional parameters depending on the function.

Save changes with the disk icon. Help is available via the question mark. With the eye icon, outputs on the model can be activated directly to facilitate assignment.

image74.png

docs.title: Dual Assignment
docs.text:
Each output can be assigned twice. Example:

  • First level: parking light & high beam
  • Second level: hazard warning lights

Switching between levels is configurable.

docs.title: Live Data
docs.text:
The live data view shows stick positions and switch states in real time. Channels can be learned and functions switched on or off.

image86.png

[[IMAGE_078]] When the channels are correctly learned, the blue dots must follow your stick movements. If you use CPPM, IBUS, or SBUS, the channels must also be correctly assigned. 665544332211 [[IMAGE_079]] Here all channels are displayed as actually measured. The learning information is not considered here, i.e., the bars can also move downwards even if the stick is moved upwards.1: A change of channel assignment should only be done if CPPM, IBUS, or SBUS is used.2-5: These values are set by the learning process. 1000 – 2000 and 1500 center position are the standard values. The values can be adjusted and saved.6: Scroll right to see the values for other channels (only relevant for CPPM, IBUS, SBUS) The right side of the live data shows the switch states. Some can be set by clicking. The three function groups basic light functions, additional functions, and analog can be used as switches in the functions, e.g., in the simple switch function. [[IMAGE_080]] [[IMAGE_081]]

image87.png

With correct learning, the blue dots follow the stick movements. For CPPM, I-BUS, or S-BUS, the channel assignment must be correct.

image88.png

The right side shows switch states, some of which can be changed by clicking.

After connecting, the ControlPanel shows available updates for bus modules.

Tips for a safe update:

  • Update only one module at a time.
  • Avoid power interruptions.
  • Disconnect servos and motors during the update.
  • Restart model and ControlPanel if problems occur.

Updates can reset factory settings. The ControlPanel automatically backs up all settings on the hard drive.

Modules can be restarted, reset to factory settings, or updated.

The assistant supports channel learning. Set the correct assignment for K1 and K2 beforehand, especially when using control pads.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
• Turn signals and cornering lights respond reversed: Channels are not calibrated.
  • Reverse light stays on when driving forward: Channels are not calibrated.
  • Steering servo turns incorrectly: Correct the rotation direction after calibration with ControlPanel. Changes in the transmitter are overwritten during calibration.
  • Light functions cannot be controlled: Check if the model is operational (battery charged, transmitter and model switched on). Check the wiring. Test the speed controller directly on the receiver without the light assistant.
  • No response after switching on: Green LED should blink twice briefly. Channels must be calibrated.
  • Calibration of channels not possible: Check connection and function of all channels with a servo. Use the LiveData assistant to verify channel movements.
  • No connection with PC: Check driver installation, model switched on and USB connected, correct COM port selected.
  • Module not found after update: Close ControlPanel, restart system, try update again. In case of problems, disconnect all modules except the affected one.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
• Turn signals and cornering lights respond reversed: Channels are not calibrated.
  • Reverse light is on when driving forward: Channels are not calibrated.
  • Steering servo rotates the wrong way: Adjust the rotation direction after calibration with ControlPanel. Changes in the transmitter will be overwritten during calibration.

  • No damaged module displayed: Disconnect all modules from the bus, restart ControlPanel, connect the problematic module and reconnect.

docs.title: Safety Instructions
docs.text:
Please read this manual carefully and pay special attention to the warnings marked in red. To avoid damage, please observe the following points:

  • Changes to the wiring may only be made when the device is switched off.
  • Ensure that no unexpected movements can occur during commissioning or configuration (e.g., remove drive wheels from the ground).
  • Always place the electronics in protected areas, especially away from moisture and oil.
  • Do not route data-carrying cables (e.g., antenna or bus cables) near power cables (motor, battery, digital servos) to avoid interference.
  • Ensure a stable and sufficient power supply for the electronics. Cheap BECs or speed controllers labeled "BEC 5V/1A" often do not provide the necessary voltage quality.
  • Replace damaged components and cables immediately.
  • Operate the components only within the technical specifications.
  • Disconnect the battery after driving, especially with LiPo or LiFe batteries, to avoid damage.
  • Avoid short circuits and reverse polarity.

Important: Do not connect the control pad to a Hall sensor controller! Opening the remote control transmitter may void the warranty. If you have any questions, please contact the manufacturer.

image3.png

docs.title: Technical Data
docs.text: Property Description Power Supply Via the channel in the remote control transmitter Max. Current Consumption 20 mA Number of Buttons 12 (deflection from 28 – 100 % in 14.5 % steps) Number of Controllable Functions 45 Supported Speed Controllers All Supported Sound Modules Servonaut, Beier Supported Remote Control Systems All (learning mode required) Long Press From Approx. 0.5 seconds Dimensions 78 x 33 mm

docs.title: Product Description
docs.text:
The control pad enables the control of all lighting functions as well as the beacon light and the saddle coupling via short tap functions (orange symbols). Additionally, functions such as trailer supports, dump bed, ramp, manual transmission, two servos, passing light, horn, all on/off, and engine start/stop can be controlled by long press (white symbols).

Up to 22 additional functions can be controlled via a layer switch (button top left), with eleven functions each accessible by short and long press.

The control pad is compatible with all remote control transmitters and occupies only one channel. Ideally, a free expansion channel is used. With modern transmitters without expansion channels, an occupied channel can also be used, but it will no longer be available for other applications.

Signal processing takes place in the central control units KLM 4/0, KLM 4/12–500, or KLM 4/16. Older KLM light assistants are also compatible with the current ControlPanel after an update and can be used without restrictions.

Commissioning is done in five steps, which are described in detail below:

  1. Installation in the remote control transmitter
  2. Configuration of the KLM
  3. Setting the modulation
  4. Learning the channels
  5. Optional: Fine adjustment

Steps 3 and 4 may need to be repeated several times until the optimal modulation is found.

docs.title: Functionality
docs.text:
The buttons can trigger different functions depending on the duration of the press:

  • Short press: Orange symbols
  • Long press: White symbols
  • Additional layer: Toggle with the top left button, enabling 22 more functions (gray symbols)

docs.title: Top Row
docs.text: Short press Long press Pad level Engine Start/Stop Left indicator Servo 1 Hazard lights Party mode Right indicator Servo 2 Shift up Flash headlights Shift down Horn

docs.title: Bottom Row
docs.text:
Short press Long press Parking light Supports up Low beam Supports down High beam Dump bed up Fog light/rear fog light Dump bed down Beacon light Ramp up Fifth wheel coupling Ramp down

Additional layer: Pressing the top left button activates the additional function layer. Starting from the bottom left and moving counterclockwise, up to 22 additional functions (eleven short/long each) can be switched.

docs.title: ControlPanel - Live Data
docs.text:
The Live Data view in the ControlPanel helps with getting to know the control pad and the controls, as all functions are clearly displayed.

image4.png

docs.title: Installation in a Carson Transmitter
docs.text:
The control pad must be connected to an analog channel; switch channels are not suitable. If the pad is connected as shown below, the right stick vertically occupies the control signals of the pad and cannot be used for other purposes.

Note: Set the trimmer to one end stop and leave it there. If the deflection is later too small, the trimmer can be set to the other end stop and the pad relearned.

image5.jpeg

Since Carson remote control systems do not have a linear control curve, the pad deflections must be set asymmetrically (see section 5.11 "Learning the channels for asymmetric deflections").

The following settings are required:

  • Press button 7 and then button 10.
  • Press button 6 and then button 2.

image6.jpeg

This optimally sets the control range. Then learn the channels accordingly.

Since Carson remote control systems vary greatly, other settings may occasionally be necessary.

docs.title: Installation in a Graupner MC20
docs.text:
image7.png

A better option than the one shown above is soldering on the circuit board, as no mechanical movements can occur here.

image9.jpeg

image10.jpeg

docs.title: Determining the Pin Assignment – Theory
docs.text:
The position determination of the controllers in remote control transmitters is usually done via standard potentiometers that work as voltage dividers. Between terminals A and B there is a carbon track on which a wiper (the so-called "root") moves. The voltage at the wiper is a portion of the total voltage between A and B.

image11.png

Example: If 5 V are applied between A and B and the stick is centered, you measure 2.5 V between A and W and 2.5 V between W and B. Moving the stick, you might measure, for example, 2 V between A and W and 3 V between W and B.

The goal is to find out which pin is the supply voltage and which is the wiper. Tip: The middle pin is often the wiper. Do not rely on cable colors but carefully measure the pin assignment.

docs.title: Determining the Pin Assignment – Practice
docs.text:
Use a multimeter and turn on the remote control transmitter. Avoid short circuits and touching other contacts to prevent damage.

Check if the multimeter test leads are connected correctly (black usually COM, red usually V). If unsure, test the polarity with a battery.

Perform the following measurements and note the values (if negative values occur, swap the test leads and measure again):

  • Pin 1 against Pin 2
  • Pin 1 against Pin 3
  • Pin 2 against Pin 3

The largest measured voltage corresponds to the supply voltage (typically 3 V, 3.3 V, or 5 V). The two smaller values add up to the largest voltage.

The brown cable is connected to the negative pole (ground), the red to the positive pole. The orange cable is connected to the remaining pin (wiper).

Now press the upper left button. A servo connected to the receiver output should move. If the deflection is too small, the control range can be adjusted later.

For a 3-pin connection with 2.54 mm pitch, the supplied connector housing can be used. Otherwise, the socket cable can be soldered and the pad plugged in.

docs.title: Setting the Control Range
docs.text:
Since the pad is universally usable for all remote control transmitters, the control range (maximum deflection) must be set once. Some transmitters internally produce only ±20 % at the controller, others require the full deflection.

The control range should be as large as possible, otherwise not all functions switch reliably or interference is amplified.

image12.png

If you are learning the pad for the first time and do not know the necessary control range, set the range to maximum by first pressing the setup button and S4 simultaneously (setup first).

Start the Kraftwerk ControlPanel and open the LiveData. If the channels are not yet learned, pressing the high beam button should show a deflection of over 70 %. The high beam button produces the maximum deflection in one direction, the low beam button the maximum deflection in the other direction.

image14.png

Check whether the high beam button (largest deflection) and the parking light button (second largest deflection) show different percentage values (difference at least 5 %). The same applies to the low beam button and the saddle clutch.

If this is not the case, the pad is overdriving the channel and the control range must be reduced.

To adjust, click the Learn button, confirm the center position by clicking again, press the high beam button, click again, press the low beam button, click Save, and you are done. (The text on the button changes with each click.)

Summary:

  • High beam and low beam buttons should produce values over 70 % (sign does not matter).
  • The difference between high beam and parking light as well as low beam and saddle clutch should be at least 5 %.
  • Optional: Headlights and fog lights (smallest deflections) should still produce visible deflections over 10 %.

docs.title: Configuration of the KLM
docs.text:
image15.png

The control pad can be connected to K1 or K2. Connect the model via USB, turn on the remote control and model, and start the ControlPanel.

Open the system settings via the gear icon or the "System" menu item. Make sure that the KLM is selected in the dropdown list at the top.

Change "Assignment K1" or "Assignment K2" to "Pad Light And Sound".

Tip: If layer control is activated for K1, the assignment for K2 is grayed out because this channel is needed for layer switching. If you want to use the pad on K2, select a different entry for K1, e.g., "Custom".

After saving, the KLM is configured for the control pad.

Tip: If you are unsure which channel the pad is connected to, the Live Data view of the ControlPanel can help.

docs.title: Configuration of the RS1
docs.text: If the corresponding jumper on the RS1 is set, the pad must be connected to channel 3. Alternatively, the pad can be connected to K1 to K3 and configured via the system settings of the KLM in the ControlPanel. The further steps correspond to those of the KLM.

docs.title: Learning the Pad on the KLM
docs.text:
There are several ways to learn the pad. The easiest is via the live data in the ControlPanel:

image14.png

  1. Click the Learn button, the text changes to "Center position".
  2. Click again to confirm the center position.
  3. Press the layer button and click again ("Top / left").
  4. Press the minus button and click again ("Bottom / right").
  5. Finally, click "Save".

Alternatively, you can use the "Learn Channels" assistant in the ControlPanel, which guides you through learning all channels. If the assignment K1/K2 is set correctly, the pad will be automatically included.

A standard method without the ControlPanel is also possible. Details can be found in the manual for the EasyBus lighting systems or the RS1.

When learning the pad, first press the layer button, then the minus button.

docs.title: Checking the Level
docs.text:
Open the Live Data Assistant. Press the parking light button; the percentage value should be around 86% (+/- 4%). Press the clutch button; the value should be around -86% (+/- 4%).

image16.png

image3.png

If one of the values is above 90%, reduce the level (Setup button and S3 or S2 or S1) and restart the channel learning process.

docs.title: Verification and Fine-Tuning
docs.text:
When the calibration and channels are correctly learned, the pad should work. Test each button and check in the live data if the corresponding checkmarks are set.

Tip: Use the live data view and initially ignore the lights/functions on the model to rule out confusion caused by programming errors.

With cheap remote controls, it may be necessary to fine-tune the evaluation of the pad signals.

image17.png

To do this, press the high beam and then the fog light button. Enter the displayed percentage values in the system settings (gear icon) of the KLM and save them.

docs.title: The pad switches functions unreliably or incorrectly
docs.text:
First, check if the KLM is configured correctly. Open the system settings and check the entries "Assignment K1" and "Assignment K2".

Press the keys in the following order and check whether the values each decrease by 14.5%, starting top left with 100, 85.5, 71, 56.5, 42, 27.5% or top right with -100, -85.5, -71, -56.5, -42, -27.5%.

image18.png

If one of the second values (85.5% / -85.5%) is greater than 90% / -90%, the output is too high. If one of the second values (30% / -30%) is less than 15%, please follow section 5.11.

Also enter the percentage values for high beam and fog lights in the system settings of the KLM (see chapter 5.9).

docs.title: The pad is connected, but no value moves in the live data
docs.text: Check whether the pad is connected correctly and the correct receiver output is used. A directly connected servo should move when the button is pressed.

docs.title: Learning the Channels for Asymmetric Deflections
docs.text:
This section is only relevant if all previous steps were performed correctly and the high beam or fog lights show too small or no deflection.

Possible causes:

  • Misadjusted center position (trimmer, subtrim)
  • Electronic channel limitation
  • Cheap remote control transmitter

image20.png

If possible, reset all settings for the used channel and start the normal learning process.

If this is not possible, the control for both deflections must be set separately.

Open the Live Data Assistant and focus on the value marked below (when using K2, the value to the left). This value changes when pressing a button and should be as close as possible to the following values:

  • Center position: 1500
  • Level button: 1000 (depending on transmitter also 2000)
  • Parking light: 1070 (depending on transmitter also 1930)
  • Minus button: 2000 (depending on transmitter also 1000)
  • Saddle coupling: 1930 (depending on transmitter also 1070)

The greater the control, the further the values move away from 1500. The deflection of the level button should be as far away as possible, but the deflection for the parking light must be clearly different. The same applies to the minus button and saddle coupling.

Now set the control for both deflections separately:

  • Press Setup+ and S4 to set the maximum control for the positive deflection.
  • Check the values for the pad button and the parking light. If these are similar (e.g., 1930 and 1925), reduce the control with Setup+ and S3 and check again. The difference should be at least 50.
  • Repeat the process for the negative deflection with Setup- and the S buttons. Check the values for the minus button and saddle coupling.

image21.png

docs.text:

docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Dear Customer,

We are pleased that you have chosen one of our products. If you have any questions or suggestions, please feel free to contact us by email at info@kraftwerk.shop or visit our website www.kraftwerk.shop. There you will always find up-to-date user manuals, software updates, and helpful videos.

We wish you much enjoyment with your new product!

docs.title: Safety Instructions
docs.text:
• Read this manual carefully, especially the warnings marked in red.
  • Changes to the wiring may only be made when the power is off.
  • Ensure that no unexpected movements can cause damage during commissioning or configuration (e.g., remove drive wheels from the ground).
  • Protect the electronics from moisture and oil.
  • Do not route data-carrying cables (e.g., antenna or bus cables) parallel to power cables (motor, battery, digital servos) to avoid interference.
  • Replace damaged components and cables immediately.
  • Operate the components only within the specified technical specifications.
  • Disconnect the battery after use, especially with LiPo batteries, to prevent deep discharge.
  • Avoid short circuits and reverse polarity.

docs.title: Software Version
docs.text: Please always download the latest version of the ControlPanel and connect the module. Updates with new features and bug fixes are applied automatically.

docs.title: Status LED
docs.text:
• Green (normal): LED blinks briefly every two seconds – normal operation.
  • Yellow (StandBy): LED blinks slower – no control signals are accepted.
  • Fast red blinking: Undervoltage – hazard lights active, motor is shut off.
  • Slow red blinking or other colors: Please contact support.

docs.title: StandBy Switch
docs.text:
With the switch, you can turn the model on or off or put it into StandBy mode. In StandBy mode, all functions continue to run, but no control signals from the remote control are accepted – this corresponds to a demo mode.

image2.jpeg

In the OFF position, controllers, servos, and bus modules are switched off. Caution: A small current flow can still cause deep discharge of the battery. Therefore, always disconnect the battery at the latest by the end of the day.

docs.title: Learning Channels
docs.text:
Learning the channels is mandatory and only needs to be done once. There are three options:

  1. Without a PC – see chapter 8.4 “Learning the Channels”
  2. With the learning assistant in the ControlPanel under System > Learn Channels
  3. Individually in the LiveData

image3.png

image4.png

docs.title: Engine Start/Stop
docs.text: Depending on the setting, it is necessary to start the engine manually. If the engine is not running audibly, the drive motor will not turn either.

docs.title: Hissing Sounds
docs.text: During driving, the brake is activated, which can be heard as hissing sounds. This is intentional and can be adjusted or disabled in the ControlPanel.

docs.title: Product Description
docs.text: The RS1 combines speed controller, sound module, and lighting system in a compact device. It connects to up to five channels of any receiver and also supports SUMD and IBUS, making up to 32 channels available in the system.

docs.title: Robust Speed Controller
docs.text:
• Continuous load capacity of 25 A
  • 32 kHz switching frequency – suitable for bell armature motors
  • Integrated clutch function and start aid
  • Future features such as gearbox support and cruise control will be unlocked via update

docs.title: Exceptional Sound
docs.text:
KraftwerK sets standards in sound programmability. Unlike competitors who allow only a few sound snippets, the RS1 can store and flexibly combine any number of sounds.

The sound is defined over an RPM range (e.g., 500 rpm at start, 2300 rpm at run-out). For different RPM ranges, sound snippets with minimum, base, and maximum RPM can be stored:

  • Minimum: Sound fades in adapted to RPM
  • Base RPM: Sound plays at full volume and 1:1 speed
  • Maximum: Sound fades out adapted to RPM

The snippets can overlap, creating an ultra-realistic soundscape. A total of four audio tracks are available:

  1. Normal acceleration
  2. Load-dependent sound (mixed proportionally or switchable)
  3. Deceleration sound (automatic when releasing the control stick)
  4. Engine brake

For beginners, six high-quality sounds are pre-installed on the module. Additionally, a sound library is offered at www.kraftwerk.shop, where premium sounds can be downloaded at low cost and stored on the module.

docs.title: Unlimited Expandability
docs.text:
Thanks to KraftwerK’s leading role in RC bus systems, the RS1 can be expanded arbitrarily via six EasyBus outputs. Lighting boards, switch expansions, servo controls, and other modules can be easily integrated.

A special highlight is four discreet sockets on the top, through which additional modules can be plugged in. The first module in this series is the BL8A, an 8A brushless controller for hydraulic pumps.

docs.title: Factory Settings
docs.text: image5.png

docs.title: Connections
docs.text:
• 5 servo inputs (Gas/Data input also supports IBUS/SBUS/SUMD, automatic detection)
  • 5 servo outputs (adjustable, factory settings see above)
  • Off/On/Standby switch
  • Standby jumper for configuring sound and control mode
  • 6 EasyBus outputs
  • I2C expansion port
  • Micro-USB port
  • SD card slot (max. 32 GB, FAT32 formatted)
  • Speaker connection (4 Ohm or higher)
  • 10A switching outputs
  • Status LED
  • 4 expansion pins for plug-in modules

image6.png

docs.title: Speed Controller
docs.text: Property Description Operating Voltage 6 - 20 V BEC 6 V / 3 A Switching Frequency Adjustable up to 32 kHz Clutch Function Yes Manual Transmission Support Yes (update planned) Inertia Simulation Optional Cruise Control Optional (update planned) Protection Functions Battery undervoltage, motor short circuit, overtemperature Servo Functions Proportional function, switched, two and three positions, saddle plate, trailer supports, motor dump/truck bed, motor ramp/platform, motor manual transmission

docs.title: Sound
docs.text:
Property Description Number of Sounds Unlimited Simultaneously Playable Sounds Approx. 10 Playback Modes Button press, loop, once, permanent, random, stop points Supported Formats WAV, MP3 Sampling Rate All (automatic conversion to 44,100 Hz) Volume Control Potentiometer, via channel, via software SD Card Max. 32 GB, formatted FAT32 (larger cards can be formatted to 32 GB)

image8.png

docs.title: Commissioning
docs.text:
The RS1 must be connected to five channels of the receiver. The use of a control pad for light and sound can be configured via jumpers.

image9.png

Right jumper set: Control pad connected to K3.

The motor brake is active as long as K2 is pressed upwards, provided the model sound supports this. If K2 is pulled down and then tapped to the right, the horn switches to horn and fanfare (if supported).

image11.png

image13.png

Upper jumper not set: Control via button on K3.

Without a control pad, the channel assignment is denser. Throttle, steering, volume, and load are controlled as with the control pad. The motor brake is designed as a memory function: a short tap activates/deactivates it, a long tap starts/stops the motor, a half tap activates the horn. The switching of the horn to horn/fanfare is done as described above.

image15.png

image17.png

When using a button on K3, only full functions can be switched. Half functions can be realized via mixers of the remote control.

image19.png

image20.png

docs.title: Connecting to the Receiver
docs.text:
image8.png

Before connecting, you should check the channel assignment of your receiver. Use a servo to test each output for proper function and note the assignment.

image22.jpeg

Note: Block the model to prevent unintended movement. Then connect all servo cables to the corresponding receiver ports. When using IBUS, SBUS, or SUMD, the RS1 automatically sets itself to the corresponding protocol. Channel assignments can be adjusted in the live data if necessary.

docs.title: Learning the Channels
docs.text:
image8.png

Learning is essential for the proper operation of the KraftwerK EasyBus systems. After any change to the remote control system (e.g., deflections, directions of rotation, center positions), the channels must be relearned.

Servos and motors controlled by KraftwerK systems must be adjusted via the ControlPanel – not through the remote control system menu!

  1. Turn on the remote control and prop up the model so that no drive wheels touch the ground.
  2. Move the throttle and steering channels to their end positions and turn on the model.
  3. The red LED lights up, confirming the learning mode.
  4. Return the sticks to the center position.

LED signals during learning:

  • 1x short red: Throttle stick full throttle forward, then full brake
  • 2x short red: Steering stick full left, then full right
  • 3x short red: Additional channel K1 full left, then full right
  • 4x short red: Additional channel K2 full up, then full down
  • 5x short red: Additional channel K3 full up, then full down

After all sticks return to the center position, the LED lights up solid red for a long time, signaling the end of the learning process. The system restarts automatically and indicates readiness with a green double blink.

Here you can switch functions in the model:

docs.title: Live Data
docs.text:
The Live Data view provides a real-time visualization of stick movements and switch states. In the lower left, the channel assignment can be adjusted when using IBUS, SBUS, or SUMD. Channels can also be individually learned here by clicking the "Learn" button and following the instructions.

image45.png

Depending on the control pad or SUMD remote control, the right view may vary.

image46.png

docs.title: Device Information
docs.text: This section displays all module-specific information, such as name, software version, sound engine version, bootloader version, number of outputs, production date, and device type.

docs.title: System Settings
docs.text:
In the system settings, basic module adjustments can be made that significantly influence the behavior of the model.

image47.png

  • Name: Display of the module in the overview – important when multiple modules are used.
  • Learning mode active: Manual learning method can be deactivated here.
  • Cut-off voltage: 6.4 V for 7.2 V NiMh/NiCd batteries, 9.6 V for 3S LiPo batteries.
  • Model number: Switch between three models.
  • Short/Long press: Defines the duration for long button presses.
  • Volume: Starting volume of the sound, automatically adjusts to potentiometer or stick movements.
  • Half deflection: Threshold for half deflection (5% to X%).
  • Full deflection: Threshold for full deflection (X% to 100%).
  • Infrared transmission power: Setting depending on the distance of the diodes (1 = small, 3 = large).
  • Motor profile: Influences starting behavior and cruise control.
  • Motor start automatic: Motor starts with slight forward stick movement.
  • Motor stop after: Time in seconds until motor stops due to inactivity.
  • Daytime running lights always on: If not set, daytime running lights are only activated with parking lights.
  • Turn signals On/Off: Pulse duration for blink speed.
  • ESC type: Currently only "Separate brake" supported.
  • Reverse driving: Hazard lights and turn signals can be linked with reverse light.
  • Brake light sensitivity and afterglow: Controls sensitivity and afterglow duration.
  • Steering type: Automatic or manual turn signal operation.
  • Turn signal threshold: When automatic turn signals switch on or reset.
  • Cornering light type: Always on or only with headlights on.
  • Motor runs immediately: Motor runs directly after switching on or must be started manually.
  • Party mode: Defines functions when pressing the party mode button on the control pad.
  • Pad light & sound: Fine tuning of the control pad channels for high beam and fog lights.
  • Hydraulic speed increase and after-run: Settings for hydraulic channels.

image48.png

docs.title: Assignment K1 – K32
docs.text:
• None: Channel is not used.
  • Pad light & sound: Use of the control pad on this channel.
  • Custom: Own assignment for half/full, short/long deflections as memory or direct function.

image49.png

docs.title: Possible Settings
docs.text:
image50.png

  • RS1 K1/K2 Standard: Factory settings for K1 to K3.
  • Sound change: Switch sounds by button press.
  • Model selector switch: Switch between models (depending on system settings).
  • StandBy switch: StandBy mode analogous to the switch on the RS1.
  • Volume: Direct volume control via remote control.
  • Hydraulics: Activates speed increase of the motor sound.

docs.title: RS1 Sound Settings
docs.text: image51.png

docs.title: Basic Settings
docs.text:
image52.png

  • Project Name: Selection of projects and included sounds.
  • RPM Range Min/Max: Defines the RPM range of the engine sound.
  • Dynamics: Determines how agile the sound reacts to changes.
  • Engine Characteristics: Maximum speed for acceleration and deceleration.
  • Turbo Characteristics: Special maximum speed for the turbo.
  • Engine Brake Switch Speed (%): Transition speed between normal state and load.
  • Default Type: Preset sound type for new sounds.
  • Load Switch Speed (%): Transition speed between normal state and engine brake.

image53.png

image54.png

Note: The sounds on the PC are in reduced quality for performance and copyright reasons. On the module, they are played back in full quality.

image55.png

docs.title: Sound Simulation on PC
docs.text:
After clicking "Engine Start," the start sound is played and the engine sound begins to run.

image56.png

The left slider controls the RPM:

  • Up: Increase RPM
  • Down: Engine decelerates or switches to engine brake

Bars 2 to 4 visualize RPM and acceleration but cannot be changed directly. A marker shows the currently active sound and its volume.

image57.png

image58.png

docs.title: Save Settings
docs.text:
• Save basic settings
  • Save sounds
  • Synchronize SD card (resets all sound settings)

docs.title: Sound Types
docs.text:
image59.png

  • Engine Sound Standard: Acceleration and continuous driving
  • Engine Sound Load: Acceleration and driving in load mode
  • Engine Sound Deceleration: Vehicle is slowing down
  • Engine Sound Engine Brake: Vehicle is slowing down, engine brake activated
  • Engine Sound Load Change: One-time sound during driving condition change
  • Engine Start/Stop: Start and stop sounds, load-dependent switchable
  • Engine False Start: False start sound
  • Turbo: Turbo whistle
  • Shift Noise: Sound during gear change
  • Air Brake On/Off: Compressed air brake when closing/opening
  • Reverse Warning: Signal when reversing
  • Horn/Horn/Fanfare: Switchable via horn
  • Additional Sound: Custom sounds with additional functions
  • Power-On Sound: After complete module start
  • Start-Up Sound: When starting off (optional)
  • Stop Sound: When stopping
  • MP3: Individual MP3s (only one MP3 playable at a time)

docs.title: Engine Sound Settings
docs.text:
image60.png

image61.png

  • Engine Sound Preset: Standard, Load, Delay, Engine Brake (checks on the right)
  • Filename on SD Card: .snd is the internal Kraftwerk format
  • Play: Play sound
  • Open: Load sound file
  • Volume: Base volume, depending on driving situation
  • Checkmarks: Define in which driving states the sound is active

image62.png

Sound Visualization: Filename and length of the sound snippet.

image63.png

  • Min RPM: RPM at which the sound is slowly faded in
  • Ref RPM: Base RPM at which the sound is played at full volume
  • Max RPM: RPM at which the sound is slowly faded out

The sounds are faded in, played, and faded out according to the RPM to create a realistic soundscape.

docs.title: Additional Sounds
docs.text:
image64.png

The left half corresponds to the engine sounds with sound type, filename, play, open, and volume. However, only two checkboxes are available here:

  • Engine Standard
  • Engine Load

These define whether the sound is played with or without load.

image65.png

docs.title: Top Buttons
docs.text:
• Trigger: 1. Rising edge, 2. Falling edge, 3. Both edges
  • Start conditions: Sound starts when the function is turned on or off, or both
  • Playback type: 1. Loop, 2. Always play, 3. Return to start or continue playing

All combinations are possible.

docs.title: Triggering Functions
docs.text:
Depending on the use of the control pad or SUMD remote control, different switches are available. The functions correspond to those in the LiveData.

image66.png

image67.png

image68.png

docs.title: Stops
docs.text:
Stop points in seconds can be defined for each sound. With the "always play" function enabled, the sound runs until the next stop point or continues if the function remains active.

image69.png

docs.title: Random
docs.text:
The sound can be set to start randomly every x to y seconds, as long as it is activated.

image70.png

docs.title: Adding a New Sound
docs.text:
Supported sound types:

  • All common sound and video types are supported.
  • The ControlPanel automatically converts to the required format including bitrate.
  • Examples: WAV, MP3, OGG, MP4, SND

To add a new sound, click the "New Sound Entry" button at the end of the list.

image71.png

A new row with the default sound type from the basic settings will be created. The file can be loaded via "Open". Alternatively, files can be dragged and dropped into the ControlPanel.

image72.png

image73.png

If a file is dragged into the sound visualization, the old sound will be replaced. For engine sounds, multiple files can be imported simultaneously. If the filenames contain a three- to five-digit number, it will be used as the "ref RPM" and the RPM range will be adjusted automatically. These ranges can later be optimized in the ControlPanel and loaded onto the module.

image74.png

  • Control Pad: Control panel mounted on the remote control transmitter for convenient operation.

docs.title: Instructions for Installing the Premium Sounds
docs.text: Company: Wolfgang Haring e.U. Company Registration Number: FN 312421 v (Regional Court Wr. Neustadt) Address: Kammanngasse 7-9A/8, A-2700 Wiener Neustadt Phone: +49 30 6098490 431

docs.title: Basics
docs.text: The term "sound module" is used below. This refers to all modules that use the KraftwerK sound engine. Currently, this applies to the models RS1, ST1, and SBM1.

docs.title: Ordering Premium Sounds
docs.text:
Premium sounds can be conveniently ordered through our online shop. In the Sounds tab, you will find the current selection of available premium sounds.

image1.png

Please note that each sound can only be installed on one sound module. For customers who want to use a sound on multiple modules, we offer attractive pricing tiers to facilitate multiple usage.

image2.png

The order is placed as usual via the shopping cart and paid online. There are no shipping costs, as the sounds are provided digitally.

docs.title: Installation on the Sound Module
docs.text:
The following prerequisites must be met for the installation:

  • The sound module is switched on and operational.
  • The module is connected to the PC.
  • At least firmware version 2024.3 is installed.

After starting, the module is automatically detected and displayed in the program.

image3.png

docs.title: Step 1: Open Sound Settings
docs.text:
Open the sound settings in the program.

image4.png

docs.title: Step 2: Start Premium Sound Manager
docs.text:
Click the Premiumsounds button to open the Premium Sound Manager.

image5.png

image6.png

docs.title: Step 3: Login
docs.text:
Enter the user data with which you purchased the premium sounds and click “Login”. All your purchases will then be displayed.

image7.png

In the right column, you will see either “Available” if the sound has not yet been assigned to a sound module, or the serial number of the module on which the sound is already installed.

docs.title: Step 4: Select and Download Sound
docs.text:
Select the desired sound and the appropriate project on the SD card. Then click “Download to the module”.

In the example below, the module is already registered, and the sound is being installed again, for example because the SD card was replaced.

image8.png

docs.title: Step 5: Confirm Download
docs.text:
Confirm the download to start the process.

image9.png

docs.title: Step 6: Installation and Licensing
docs.text:
After confirmation, the selected sound is downloaded. The license file is either newly generated or an existing license file is reused. Both files are then copied to the sound module.

image10.png

docs.title: Step 7: Reload Sound
docs.text:
After the copying process, the sound is automatically reloaded.

image11.png

docs.title: Step 8: Sound Ready for Use
docs.text:
The sound is now fully installed and ready for use.

image12.png

docs.title: Notes and Tips
docs.text:
• Firmware Update: Make sure your sound module is always operated with the latest firmware version to avoid compatibility issues.
  • Multiple Installation: If you want to use a sound on multiple modules, check the available price tiers before ordering.
  • Changing the SD Card: When replacing the SD card, the sound must be installed again as described in step 4.
  • Support: If you have any problems or questions, our customer service is available by phone or email.

docs.title: RCMultiBus – Overview and Functionality
docs.text:
Version: 1.05 Date: 11.01.2021 Author: Wolfgang Haring e.U. Company Register Number: FN 312421 v (Regional Court Wr. Neustadt) Address: Kammanngasse 7-9A/8, A-2700 Wiener Neustadt

image1.png

image2.png

docs.title: General
docs.text:
The RCMultiBus is a superior protocol for all 3-wire data buses used in the RC sector. A slave device that supports RCMultiBus must be able to interpret all bus types including protocol, baud rate, and polarity. A master device, on the other hand, only needs to be able to generate one bus type.

The currently supported bus types are:

  • IBUS
  • SBUS
  • EX-Bus
  • CPPM

Depending on the bus type, proportional and/or digital information can be received. Each proportional channel is always converted into digital switch functions (permanent and in memory).

docs.title: Bus Types and Channel Values
docs.text:
Each channel value is stored and can be individually mapped to servo or motor outputs. Additionally, the movement of each channel is monitored, from which a bitfield is generated. This bitfield has three dimensions:

  • Direction: Top / Bottom
  • Movement Range: Full / Half
  • Memory Status: Memory / Permanent
Channel Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full CH1 Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full CH2 Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full … CHx Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full

docs.title: Examples of Bitfield Flags
docs.text:
• Example 1: Channel 4 is moved to +30% → Flag CH4 Permanent/Top/Half is set.
  • Example 2: Channel 4 is moved back within half a second → Flag CH4 Permanent/Top/Half is cleared, Flag CH4 Memory/Top/Half is set.
  • Example 3: Channel 1 is moved to -100% → Flag CH1 Permanent/Bottom/Full is set.
  • Example 4: Channel 1 is moved back after one second → Flag CH1 Permanent/Bottom/Full is cleared, Flag CH1 Memory/Bottom/Full is set.

docs.title: Multiswitch Systems (Futaba, Graupner)
docs.text:
Multiswitch systems are slower because the signals are transmitted multiplexed sequentially and mainly use buttons or switches (no sticks). Therefore, the states “short” and “half” cannot be used here.

Channel Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full CH1 Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full CH2 Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full … CHx Memory Short Memory Long Permanent Top Full Top Half Bottom Half Bottom Full

docs.title: SUMD and SUMDV3
docs.text:
The use of SUMD or SUMDV3 depends on the version of the transmitter/receiver as well as the firmware of the receiver.

  • SUMD: Works analogously to IBUS, SBUS, EX-Bus, and CPPM (see above).
  • SUMDV3: Transmits a field of so-called "digital switches" that are received and stored by RCMultiBus modules. Here, both channel-dependent switches as in IBUS, SBUS, EX-Bus, CPPM as well as SUMDV3-specific digital switches can be used.

docs.title: PWM Standard
docs.text: Works similarly to IBUS, SBUS, EX-Bus, and CPPM, but with only one channel (CH1).

docs.title: UART Settings
docs.text:
• Standard: 38,400 baud, 8 bytes, LSB first, no parity, 1 stop bit, timeout 100 µs
  • Future: 100,000 baud (planned)

docs.title: Central Time-Controlled Functions
docs.text:
Central time-controlled functions such as blinkers are controlled by two digital functions:

  • Information whether the function is generally on or off
  • Pulse that switches the lamp on and off

To control the blinker, it is sufficient to set the pulse. The general on/off information can be used for mixers or other switches (e.g., switching to a second configuration when the blinker is active).

docs.title: Basic Functions (Tag 0x01)
docs.text:
Basic functions are encoded in 5 bytes:

Offset Bit 7 Bit 6 Bit 5 Bit 4 Bit 3 Bit 2 Bit 1 Bit 0 0 Hazard lights Left indicator Right indicator High beam flasher Fog lights High beam Low beam Parking lights 1 Support legs up Fifth wheel Engine running Starting Horn Rear fog light Brake light Reverse light 2 Right indicator pulse Beacon light Daytime running light Left cornering light Right cornering light Dump down Dump up Support legs down 3 Internal/RFU Signal horn Gear down Gear up Left curve light Right curve light Internal/RFU Left indicator pulse 4 Servo 2 Servo 1 Gear 2/2 Gear 1/2 Pad level Ramp down Ramp up Internal/RFU

docs.title: Additional Functions (Tag 0x05)
docs.text: Offset Bit 7 Bit 6 Bit 5 Bit 4 Bit 3 Bit 2 Bit 1 Bit 0 0 CH1 Memory Half Short Right CH1 Memory Half Short Left CH1 Right Full CH1 Left Full CH1 Memory Full Long Right CH1 Memory Full Long Left CH1 Memory Full Short Right CH1 Memory Full Short Left 1 CH2 Memory Full Long Right CH2 Memory Full Long Left CH2 Memory Full Short Right CH2 Memory Full Short Left CH1 Right Half CH1 Left Half CH1 Memory Half Long Right CH1 Memory Half Long Left 2 CH2 Right Half CH2 Left Half CH2 Memory Half Long Right CH2 Memory Half Long Left CH2 Memory Half Short Right CH2 Memory Half Short Left CH2 Right Full CH2 Left Full … … 45 CH31 Memory Half Short Right CH31 Memory Half Short Left CH31 Right Full CH31 Left Full CH31 Memory Full Long Right CH31 Memory Full Long Left CH31 Memory Full Short Right CH31 Memory Full Short Left 46 CH32 Memory Full Long Right CH32 Memory Full Long Left CH32 Memory Full Short Right CH32 Memory Full Short Left CH31 Right Half CH31 Left Half CH31 Memory Half Long Right CH31 Memory Half Long Left 47 CH32 Right Half CH32 Left Half CH32 Memory Half Long Right CH32 Memory Half Long Left CH32 Memory Half Short Right CH32 Memory Half Short Left CH32 Right Full CH32 Left Full

docs.title: Proportional Functions (Tag 0x41)
docs.text:
The values are 16-bit signed values in the range of -500 to +500, corresponding to a display from -100% to +100%. The data is in little-endian format.

CH1 (Throttle) CH2 (Steering) CH3 CH4 … CH31 CH32

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
All commands start with 0xA1 or 0xA2, are encoded as Tag-Length-Value (TLV), and end with a XOR checksum.

Start byte Tag (Command) Length Value (Data) Checksum 0xA1 / 0xA2 0x6C 0x03 0x01 0x01 0x01 0xCF

The checksum is calculated as XOR over all bytes with 0xAA as the start value.

docs.title: SET_FCT_DIRECT – Direct Function Control
docs.text:
This function allows individual functions to be switched on or off directly.

Start byte Tag (Command) Length Value (Data) Checksum 0xA1 0x6C 0x03 0x01 0x03 0x01 0xCD

docs.title: Data Format
docs.text: Byte 1 Byte 2 Byte 3 Data object (e.g., basic functions) Bit offset (1–40, e.g., parking light, low beam) On/Off (1 = On, 0 = Off)

docs.title: SET_PARAMETER – Parameter Change
docs.text:
This function allows script parameters for all outputs to be changed, e.g., "Set all high beam brightness values to 80%."

Start byte Tag (Command) Length Value (Data) Checksum 0xA1 0x76 0x02 0x04 0x50 0x81

docs.title: SET_DATA – Periodic Data Transmission
docs.text:
This function is used to periodically set all values – both proportional and digital functions.

Start byte Tag of Data Structure Length Value (Data) Checksum 0xA2 corresponding tag 0x05 0x87 0x80 0x00 0x00 0x00 0xCF

docs.title: Beta2025.1: ControlPanel (07/08/2025) [151.0MB] ... download
docs.text: PremiumSounds available again, now also with order number instead of password Infrared switchable, inertia switchable Channels and functions can be named or renamed via system settings Sequencer prepared Virtual pad prepared New firmware for servo inputs (C007 issue) RS1 occasionally blinking purple and refusing service fixed Outputs were not displayed: fixed Servo twitching for SBUS: fixed If SBUS is used and no "RS1 PWM inputs (internal)" are displayed, please switch throttle/data to a normal receiver output, restart and reconnect. Then this module will also be found and updated.

docs.title: 01 RS1
docs.text: por Wolfgang · 13 de mayo de 2026

docs.title: RS1 se queda atascado en un bucle al iniciar.
docs.text:
Si el RS1 se reinicia continuamente en un bucle, generalmente se debe a un firmware muy antiguo combinado con una tarjeta SD defectuosa.

Pasos para solucionar

  1. Cambiar la tarjeta SD (máx. 32 GB, se recomienda FAT32)
  2. Instalar la versión más reciente del ControlPanel
  3. Copiar los contenidos de Dokumente\KraftwerK\ControlPanel\sdcard_original a la nueva tarjeta SD
  4. Reiniciar el RS1
  5. Reiniciar el ControlPanel
  6. Realizar todas las actualizaciones disponibles

docs.title: RS1 interrumpe las actualizaciones – ¿por qué?
docs.text:
Si una actualización no se completa, a menudo falta el directorio de firmware en la tarjeta SD.

Cómo solucionar el problema

  1. Abrir la tarjeta SD directamente en el PC o en el ControlPanel bajo Configuración de sonido → Abrir tarjeta SD
  2. Comprobar si existe una carpeta Firmware en la tarjeta SD
  3. Si no existe: crear una nueva carpeta Firmware
  4. Reiniciar el RS1
  5. Reiniciar el ControlPanel

docs.title: No se pueden instalar los Premium Sounds – ¿qué pasa?
docs.text:
Actualmente hay un problema en el servidor que impide cargar y activar los Premium Sounds.

Estado

  • El problema es conocido
  • Se está trabajando activamente en ello
  • Una próxima versión del ControlPanel solucionará el error
